content.full_text:
California
SUPERSEDED
Fair Political
cyq Io
Practices Commission
March 26,
1993
Fred S.
Etheridge
Meyers,
Nave,
Riback
& Silver
A Professional Law Corporation
Gateway Plaza
777 Davis Street,
Suite 300
San Leandro,
California
94577
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—93-075
Dear Mr.
Etheridge:
You have requested advice on behalf of Windsor Planning
Commissioners Wick,
Pollard and Procopenko regarding their duties
under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act
(the “Act”)
1
.
QUESTIONS
1)
Under the Act,
may Commissioners Wick, Pollard and
Procopenko participate in the decision regarding the mini
warehouse proj ect?
2)
If they may not participate,
may the rule of necessity be
invoked in order for a quorum to be present for the vote on the
proj ect?
CONCLUSIONS
1)
Commissioners Wick,
Pollard and Procopenko may not
participate in any governmental decision concerning the mini
warehouse since it is within 300 feet of the common property in
which,
as members of the homeowner’s association,
each holds an
undivided interest, unless there will be no financial effect on
the fair market value of the commissioners’ interest in the common
property.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A-93-075
Page
2
2)
The Act permits an otherwise disqualified public official
to fully participate in a decision to the extent that the
official’s participation is legally required.
However,
participation is limited to an open meeting or to an authorized
closed session.
FACTS
The Windsor Planning Commission is a five-member body.
Three
planning commissioners are residents of the Lakewood Hills
subdivision, which is a mixture of single family residences and
town homes in a planned unit development.
The project contains
over 20 acres of lakes and common areas.
Common property within
the subdivision is held by the Lakewood Hills Homeowners’
Association (“association”),
a non—profit mutual benefit
corporation created pursuant to a California law.
Each property
owner within the Lakewood Hills subdivision,
including the three
commissioners, has an undivided interest in the common property.
Commissioners Wick, Pollard and Procopenko each own a parcel
of real property within the Lakewood Hills subdivision, which is
their principal place of residence.
The planning commission will be meeting for a final design
review approval for a mini warehouse project in a shopping center
which is adjacent to and within 300 feet of the common property of
the association.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate
family,
or on:
*****
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
Commissioners Wick, Pollard and Procopenko each have an
ownership interest in residential property which exceeds $1,000.
An interest in real property is indirectly involved in and
materially affected by a decision if any part of the real property
is located within a 300 foot radius of the boundaries of the
property which is the subject of the decision, unless the decision


Our File No.
A-93-075
Page
3
will have no financial effect upon the official’s real property
interest.
(Regulation 18702.3(a) (1),
copy enclosed.)
We have previously advised that where a public official
owns a residential unit in and is a member of a stock cooperative
(Haas Advice Letter,
No.
A-92-366)
or where a public official owns
a condominium in a condominium complex
(Jones Advice Letter,
No.
A—90-715), that the public official has a real property
interest in the common or community areas.
In this situation,
each commissioner/residential property
owner has an undivided interest in the association’s common
property,
which is within 300 feet of the mini warehouse.
Since
any effect on the value of real property is considered material
and we do not have any facts to indicate that there will be no
effect on the value of the common property, the commissioners may
not participate in the mini warehouse decision.
Public Generally
You have requested advice under Regulation 18703.1,
which
sets forth a limited exception and provides that the effect of a
governmental decision on the principal residence of a public
official is not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally if certain conditions are met.
In order to apply this
regulation, all the criteria must be met.
Since each commissioner
has an undivided interest in the common property which is within
300 feet of the mini warehouse, condition
(a) (5)
is not met.
Therefore, the exception in this regulation does not apply.
Legally Required Participation
Section 87101 provides
a limited exception where an otherwise
disqualified public official may still participate in a decision.
Section 87101 provides:
Section 87100 does not prevent any public of
ficial from making or participating in the making
of a governmental decision to the extent his
participation is legally required for the action or
decision to be made.
The exception in Section 87101 has been narrowly interpreted
to permit the participation of the fewest financially interested
persons possible in any decision.
(In re Hudson
(1978)
4
FPPC
Ops.
13;
Schectman Advice Letter,
No.
A-92—l98..)
Consequently,
Regulation 18701 provides in pertinent part:
(a)
A public official is not legally required
to make or to participate in the making of a
governmental decision within the meaning of Govern
ment Code Section 87101 unless there exists no
alternative source of decision consistent with the


Our File No.
A-93-075
Page
4
purposes and terms of the statute authorizing the
decision.
***
(c)
This regulation shall be construed narrowly,
and shall:
(1)
Not be construed to permit an of
ficial,
who is otherwise disqualified under
Government Code Section 87100, to vote to
break a tie.
(2)
Not be construed to allow a member
of any public agency,
who is otherwise
disqualified under Government Code Section
87100,
to vote if a quorum can be convened of
other members of the agency who are not
disqualified under Government Code Section
87100,
whether or not such other members are
actually present at the time of the
disqualification.
Thus,
where only a single member is needed to make a quorum,
only one disqualified member is permitted to participate in the
decision.
This is because “the purposes of the Act are best
served by a rule which minimizes participation in government
decisions by officials with a conflict of interest.”
(In re
Hudson,
supra.)
Whenever an official makes or participates in making a
decision on the basis that participation is legally required, the
official may fully participate.
However, the official must:
1.
Disclose the existence of the conflict;
2.
Describe the nature of his or her financial interest;
3.
State the reason there is no alternative source of
decision;
and
4.
Limit participation to an open meeting or to a closed
session authorized by Sections 11126,
54956.7,
54956.8,
54956.9,
54957 or 54957.6.
(Regulation 18701(b).)


Our File No.
A-93-075
Page
5
I trust this answers your questions.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
By:
Co
sel,
Legal Division
JM:JS:dl
enc.
